title: Roadmap derivation produces false `complete` when positioned-task coverage is partial — no signal that the rollup is structurally incomplete
description:
The roadmap derivation computes milestone/phase completeness solely from positioned tasks (task_positioned_in_phase edges). A phase whose `intent` field names four members but has only one positioned task — three gaps named in prose, never decomposed into tasks with positioning edges — derives `rollup: complete` (1 positioned task, 1 complete). The stored statuses (`phase: in-progress`, `milestone: planned`) are the author's honest judgment that the work is not done. The roadmap view presents `{rollup: complete, status: planned}` as contradictory raw data with no disambiguation: the consumer cannot tell whether the stored status is stale (a divergence to repair) or the derivation is structurally incomplete (the stored status is truthful). The FEAT-011 derived-status invariant class, built on the premise that rollup truth stored status, would classify this as a divergence to repair — compounding the error.

Triggering instance: MILE-005 / PHASE-M5-WRITE-INTEGRITY on 2026-07-08. Phase intent names TASK-027, FGAP-093, FGAP-091, FGAP-085 as members. Only TASK-027 has a task_positioned_in_phase edge. The roadmap derived `rollup: complete` while the stored statuses (phase: in-progress, milestone: planned) were truthful. An LLM presented with both values defaulted to 'stored status is stale' — the predictable error when the system's own invariant class primes that interpretation.
impact: Roadmap consumers (LLMs, HTML views, agent context composition) receive a false completeness verdict with no signal of partiality. The gap between `intent` and positioning is a common state in active development — phases are authored with their intended scope before all members are decomposed into positioned tasks. The derivation's false `complete` will mislead every downstream consumer until fixed. The user must manually cross-verify every roadmap completeness claim, which defeats the substrate's purpose.

evidence.reference: PHASE-M5-WRITE-INTEGRITY: intent names 4 members (TASK-027, FGAP-093, FGAP-091, FGAP-085); only TASK-027 has a task_positioned_in_phase edge — 3/4 members are unpositioned
MILE-005: stored status 'planned', roadmap rollup 'complete' — contradiction with no disambiguation
Only 1 task_positioned_in_phase edge for PHASE-M5-WRITE-INTEGRITY (TASK-027), 0 for the 3 gap-named members
Roadmap derivation aggregates positioned tasks only; phase intent is not consumed — no partiality signal exists in the derivation path

proposed_resolution: When a phase's `intent` field names members that lack positioning edges (task_positioned_in_phase), the roadmap rollup degrades to a `partial` bucket distinct from `todo`/`in_progress`/`complete`/`blocked` — signalling 'positioning coverage is incomplete, the completeness verdict is underdetermined.' The `partial` bucket carries metadata naming the unpositioned members so the consumer can see what's missing. When positioning coverage is complete (every intent member has an edge), the rollup computes as today. The degrade is a derivation signal, not a status mutation: the stored status is untouched. This eliminates the false `complete` at the source — `{rollup: partial, status: planned}` is honest and consistent, and the derived-status invariant never flags a `partial` rollup as a divergence to repair.
